summary: Gruber builds on Tantek Çelik's analysis of cognitive load in user interfaces, proposing a refined counting methodology. While Çelik counts individual actions like clicks and keystrokes, Gruber argues that what really matters is the number of distinct UI elements a user must 'deal with.' He illustrates this by comparing instant messaging to email, then applies it devastatingly to iCal's event entry (10 elements) versus Backpack's (2 elements). The post argues that raw keystroke counts are misleading — iCal and Backpack differ by only 5 keystrokes, yet the experience is dramatically different. Gruber concludes that counting UI elements you must deal with better captures the actual felt quality of an interface.
key_points: Cognitive load in UI comes more from the number of distinct elements (nouns) you must deal with than from the number of actions (verbs) you perform
Even skipping past an irrelevant field like CC or Subject carries some cognitive load — it's not nothing, even if it's small
Raw keystroke and click counts are misleading metrics for UI quality; iCal and Backpack differ by only 5 keystrokes but feel worlds apart
The right case to optimize for is the experienced repeat user, not the first-time user who benefits from explicit labeled fields
The 'deal with it' counting method — enumerating distinct UI elements requiring attention — better reflects the actual appeal of a design
money_quotes: Çelik's technique is to count the actions, or, if you will, the verbs. But I suspect it's the nouns that add the most to the cognitive load.
My counting methodology can be reduced to the form 'Deal with some specific user interface element'.

This is not about 'easy' vs. 'hard', which are clumsy, imprecise words for describing a user interface. Easy what? Easy to learn? Easy to understand? Easy to remember how to use? Easy meaning 'simple'?

There's nothing complex or confusing about iCal's event entry UI. And with specific fields for each item of data, it is more obvious than Backpack's — but only for a first-time user, which is the wrong case to optimize for.
It's as palpable as the difference between going uphill and downhill.
Counting the user interface elements you have to deal with produces a comparison that reflects the actual appeal of the two designs.
Imagine if there were five CC fields to skip in every message window. Or 100. It's a little tiny thing, but it is not nothing.
key_insight: The cognitive load of a UI is best measured not by counting keystrokes or actions, but by counting the number of distinct interface elements a user must mentally deal with.
